content:
# Ruth Chapter 4 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), page 882 | **Source Image:** RUT_004_page_001.jpg

---

**1** Now Boaz went up to the gate and sat down there. And behold, the kinsman-redeemer of whom Boaz had spoken was passing by. And he said, "Turn aside, sit down here, So-and-so." And he turned aside and sat down.

**2** And he took ten men of the elders of the city and said, "Sit down here." And they sat down.

**3** And he said to the kinsman-redeemer, "The portion of the field that belonged to our brother Elimelech — Naomi, who has returned from the fields of Moab, is selling it.

**4** And I said, 'I will disclose it to your ear, saying: Acquire it before those sitting here and before the elders of my people. If you will redeem it, redeem. But if you will not redeem, tell me, that I may know, for there is no one besides you to redeem, and I am after you.'" And he said, "I will redeem it."

**5** And Boaz said, "On the day you acquire the field from the hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of the dead, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance."

**6** And the kinsman-redeemer said, "I am not able to redeem it for myself, lest I impair my own inheritance. Redeem for yourself what I would redeem, for I am not able to redeem it."

**7** Now this was the custom formerly in Israel concerning redemption and concerning exchange, to confirm any matter: a man would draw off his sandal and give it to his neighbor. And this was the manner of attestation in Israel.

**8** And the kinsman-redeemer said to Boaz, "Acquire it for yourself." And he drew off his sandal.

**9** And Boaz said to the elders and all the people, "You are witnesses this day that I have acquired all that belonged to Elimelech, and all that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon, from the hand of Naomi.

**10** And also Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, I have acquired as my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his place. You are witnesses this day."

**11** And all the people who were in the gate, and the elders, said, "We are witnesses. May the LORD make the woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and like Leah, the two of them who built the house of Israel. And may you act worthily in Ephrathah and be renowned in Bethlehem.

**12** And may your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, through the offspring which the LORD will give you from this young woman."

**13** And Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. And he went in to her, and the LORD gave her conception, and she bore a son.

**14** And the women said to Naomi, "Blessed be the LORD, who has not left you this day without a kinsman-redeemer. And may his name be renowned in Israel.

**15** And he shall be to you a restorer of life and a sustainer of your old age, for your daughter-in-law, who loves you, has borne him — she who is better to you than seven sons."

**16** And Naomi took the child and laid him on her bosom, and she became his guardian.

**17** And the neighbor women gave him a name, saying, "A son has been born to Naomi." And they called his name Obed. He is the father of Jesse, the father of David.

**18** Now these are the generations of Perez: Perez begot Hezron,

**19** and Hezron begot Ram, and Ram begot Amminadab,

**20** and Amminadab begot Nahshon, and Nahshon begot Salmon,

**21** and Salmon begot Boaz, and Boaz begot Obed,

**22** and Obed begot Jesse, and Jesse begot David.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **פְּלֹנִי אַלְמֹנִי (peloni almoni), v. 1** — "So-and-so," the Hebrew idiom for an unnamed person. The narrator deliberately withholds the nearer kinsman's name — he who refuses the duty of redemption is consigned to anonymity, while the name of the faithful redeemer, Boaz, endures.

2. **גֹּאֵל (go'el), vv. 1, 3, 4, 6, 8, 14** — "Kinsman-redeemer," maintained throughout. This chapter brings the go'el institution to its legal climax at the city gate. The term appears in v. 14 applied to the newborn child — the redeemer line continues.

3. **וְגַם (vegam) Ruth the Moabitess, v. 5** — Boaz introduces Ruth as an additional condition. The Leningrad Codex reads קָנִיתִי with qere/ketiv. The legal coupling of land redemption and levirate-like marriage obligation is the narrative's hinge.

4. **שָׁלַף נַעֲלוֹ (shalaf na'alo), vv. 7-8** — The sandal-removal ceremony is explained by the narrator as an archaic custom, suggesting the text was composed (or finalized) at a time when this practice required explanation. Compare Deuteronomy 25:9, where the ceremony carries a different valence.

5. **רָחֵל וּלְאָה (Rachel and Leah), v. 11** — The people's blessing invokes the matriarchs who "built the house of Israel." Rachel is named first despite being the younger wife — perhaps reflecting her prominence in popular memory, or her connection to Bethlehem (where she was buried).

6. **פֶּרֶץ (Perez), v. 12** — The blessing links this union to Judah and Tamar (Genesis 38), another story of a woman securing the continuation of a family line through unconventional means. The genealogy in vv. 18-22 confirms the connection, running from Perez to David.

7. **מֵשִׁיב נֶפֶשׁ (meshiv nefesh), v. 15** — "Restorer of life," literally "one who causes the soul/life to return." The women's blessing to Naomi undoes the bitterness of 1:20-21.

8. **The genealogy (vv. 18-22)** — Ten generations from Perez to David, forming a complete toledot ("generations") formula. This links Ruth's story into the larger arc of Israelite history, culminating in the Davidic monarchy.

— LOGOS
